results.possibleIdentity: Megan Bierman is a multi-disciplinary improvising artist, musician, and dancer who was active in the experimental art and performance scenes of the late 1960s and 1970s. She attended Bennington College from 1968 to 1972, where she studied and performed with the Judith Dunn - Bill Dixon Dance Company, a ground-breaking improvisational group. During this period, she was involved in various avant-garde projects, including performances with the Bill Dixon Ensemble in 1969. In the 1970s, she collaborated on the project "Megacycles" with fellow Bennington graduate Laurel Sprigg, Joe Sabella, and her long-term artistic partner, John Gruntfest. Her work often crosses the boundaries of music (she has played the tenor saxophone for over 40 years), dance, and visual arts (painting and drawing). Bierman is the daughter of prominent San Francisco activists Arthur and Sue Bierman; her father was a philosophy professor and her mother served on the San Francisco Board of Supervisors. She has spent much of her later career as an artist-in-residence at the Buen Dia Family School in San Francisco (since 1993) and continues to be involved in the San Francisco experimental music and performance community, appearing in events at the Center for New Music. Her early professional activity is also reflected in copyright entries from 1972 and 1973 for dramatic works and music.

webSearchQueries: "Megan Bierman" Experiments in Art and Technology

segment.text: She attended Bennington College from 1968 to 1972, where she studied and performed with the Judith Dunn - Bill Dixon Dance Company, a ground-breaking improvisational group.
In the 1970s, she collaborated on the project \"Megacycles\" with fellow Bennington graduate Laurel Sprigg, Joe Sabella, and her long-term artistic partner, John Gruntfest.
Her work often crosses the boundaries of music (she has played the tenor saxophone for over 40 years), dance, and visual arts (painting and drawing).
